does: This paper presents XGBoost-Forget, a machine unlearning method specifically designed for gradient boosting models rather than deep neural networks. The approach enables removal of specific training data points from trained XGBoost models without full retraining, maintaining near-original predictive performance while achieving computational efficiency. The method is evaluated on two tabular network intrusion detection datasets—IoT-23 and GeNIS—using metrics that measure model accuracy, unlearning speed, and quality of data removal. The core contribution is demonstrating that effective unlearning can be achieved in tree-based ensemble models commonly used for cybersecurity applications, addressing a previously neglected setting in the machine unlearning literature.
connects: Machine unlearning emerged as a response to data privacy regulations and the right to be forgotten, with prior work concentrated almost exclusively on deep learning models and vision tasks. This paper extends the machine unlearning paradigm to gradient boosting methods, which dominate practical applications in tabular data and cybersecurity domains. Network intrusion detection has traditionally relied on tree-based classifiers rather than neural networks due to their interpretability and performance on structured data. By bridging these two areas, the work acknowledges that unlearning techniques developed for convolutional or fully-connected architectures cannot directly transfer to XGBoost's tree ensemble structure, requiring domain-specific algorithmic adaptations.
enables: This work opens pathways for privacy-compliant machine learning in security-critical applications where tabular data and boosted tree models are standard. It enables organizations to honor data deletion requests in intrusion detection systems without discarding months or years of model training, reducing the operational burden of privacy compliance. The approach could extend to other tree-based applications beyond cybersecurity, such as fraud detection, credit scoring, and clinical prediction systems that similarly depend on XGBoost. Future research could explore adaptive unlearning techniques that incrementally remove data as privacy requests arrive, develop theoretical guarantees for unlearning quality in ensemble models, and apply similar methods to other gradient boosting frameworks.
